note: Interactive drill banks. Client-side is fine: drills are coached practice and answers/feedback are meant to be shown. Distinct from the quiz banks. Types: order (arrange in sequence), match (pair left to right), pick (scenario choice with feedback). Readiness is scored on first-try correctness; below readinessPass the quiz stays locked. Not MDM-exclusive: F1 (Foundation domain) shares this same map.

items.prompt: Put the FRC season in order, from first to last.
sequence: Kickoff, the new game is revealed
Build season, design and build the robot

items.prompt: Match each word to what it means.
right: The part where the robot drives itself with code
A group of teams playing together
One short game, about two and a half minutes

items.prompt: Match each training step to what you do in it.
right: Read and learn the topic
Practice like a game until you are ready
Prove you learned it at 90 percent
Use the skill for real

items.prompt: About how long is build season?
items.feedback: You get roughly six weeks to design, build, and program the robot.

items.prompt: What score do you need to pass a quiz in this track?
items.feedback: You need 90 percent, so the quiz really shows you learned it.

items.prompt: Drag the design process into the correct order.
Match each step to its job.
right: State the job in one sentence
Build something crude to answer a question
Choose an option and write down why
Turn the choice into a clean CAD model

items.prompt: "The mount must bolt to the frame." Is this a demand or a goal?
options: A demand, it must be true or the design fails
A goal, it would be nice to have
items.feedback: A demand is pass or fail. "Must" is the tell.

items.prompt: You just built a cardboard mockup and it works. What is next?
options: Select the concept and record why
items.feedback: Prototyping answers a question, then you select with a reason before detailing.

items.prompt: Two concepts both meet every demand. What decides between them?
items.feedback: Demands are pass/fail; goals break the tie between options that already pass.

items.prompt: Match each line to what it means.
right: A visible edge
A hidden edge
A center or axis

items.prompt: Third-angle layout: match each view to where it sits.
right: Above the front view
To the right of the front view

items.prompt: A callout reads 0.266 diameter THRU. What is it?
options: A hole 0.266 across, all the way through
items.feedback: Diameter symbol means across; THRU means all the way through.

items.prompt: A hole has a size callout but no dimensions to any edge. What is wrong?
options: Its position is undefined, so it cannot be made right
items.feedback: Every feature needs both a size and a location.

items.prompt: Where do you find the part's material?
items.feedback: Material, scale, units, and default tolerance all live in the title block.

items.prompt: Match each nut to its job.
right: Resists vibration on pivots
Has a built-in biting washer
Presses into material too thin to tap

items.prompt: Match each part to why it is that way.
right: Locks a gear so it turns with the shaft
Lets a bearing ride on it
Preloads the joint for stiffness

items.prompt: Which is a current high-performance motor?
items.feedback: Brushless is the standard now. The CIM is obsolete.

items.prompt: Which fastener family is the structural workhorse?
items.feedback: And 10-32 especially, since most COTS parts are threaded 10-32.

items.prompt: Two 20 DP gears have 24 and 60 teeth. Center distance?
Match each hole type to when you use it.
right: Screw passes through to a nut
Screw threads straight into the part

items.prompt: Match each fastener choice to its use.
right: A vibrating pivot
A fast one-sided joint
The highest structural loads

items.prompt: You fasten into a thick block with no room for a nut. Which hole?
items.feedback: No nut, so the threads must live in the part.

items.prompt: What does the Hole Wizard do that a sketched circle does not?
options: Sizes the hole and carries the callout to the drawing
items.feedback: Always use the Hole Wizard for real fastener holes.

items.prompt: An 8-32 clearance hole is what size?
items.feedback: Clearance is slightly larger than the screw so it passes freely.

items.prompt: Match each fit to a real example.
right: A shaft spinning in a bearing
A bearing pressed into a plate

items.prompt: Match each GD and T idea to what it means.
right: Sets a geometric control, a zone, and datums
A reference feature others are measured from
Where a hole must sit

items.prompt: A dimension with no stated tolerance is...
items.feedback: No tolerance does not mean perfect, it means the default applies.

items.prompt: Why not put a tight tolerance on every dimension?
options: Tight tolerances waste shop time, so tolerance only what needs it
items.feedback: Tolerance the interfaces that matter, leave the rest at default.

items.prompt: For a laser-cut slot and tab, how do you get a good fit?
options: Open the slot ~0.005 and shrink the tab ~0.005
items.feedback: A little clearance lets the parts actually assemble.
